3A_Bibliographic.citation: Rauschecker, A. M., Nedelec, P., Pan, S., Olaru, M., Nillo, R. M., Palmer, C. E., Pecheva, D., Dale, A. M., Jernigan, T. L., & Sugrue, L. P. (2025). Neurocognitive and brain structure correlates of reading and television habits in early adolescence. Scientific Reports, 15, 6235.
3A_Bibliographic.notes: Rauschecker and Nedelec contributed equally; Jernigan and Sugrue contributed equally as senior authors.

3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: In the current study, we analyzed 8,125 participants (mean age 9.93y, 3,854 female, Table 1) out of the 11,875 in the ABCD study 3.0 data release after filtering for imaging quality control, missing data, and behavioral outliers.

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Daily hours spent pleasurable reading and daily hours spent television viewing
3C_Exposure.exposure_instrument: Parental questionnaires (reading: hours per week converted to daily; TV: ABCD Screen Time Questionnaire weighted average of weekday/weekend)
3C_Exposure.operationalization: Continuous (hours/day); reading was parent-reported hours/week divided by 7; TV viewing was categorical converted to pseudo-continuous weighted weekday/weekend average
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: Daily hours spent reading for pleasure or television viewing were derived from parental questionnaires and coded as pseudo-continuous variables. For reading, participants' caregivers were asked to report the number of hours per week that their child engaged in pleasurable reading. For consistency with daily television viewing, this number was divided by seven to convert it to daily hours spent reading. For television viewing behavior, participants were asked in a survey to indicate from seven potential answers ('None', '< 30 minutes', '30 minutes', '1 hour', '2 hours', '3 hours', and '4 + hours') how much time they spent viewing television shows or movies on a typical weekday and on a typical weekend day.

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Neurocognitive performance (NIH Toolbox Cognition Battery) and brain cortical surface area/thickness
3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: NIH Toolbox Cognition Battery (7 subtests, 3 composite scores) and FreeSurfer-derived cortical surface area and thickness from structural MRI
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: Neurocognitive performance was assessed using the well-validated NIH Toolbox Cognition Battery that evaluates neurocognitive performance using seven different sub-tests covering cognitive domains such as language, executive function, working/episodic memory, and processing speed. The toolbox also includes three composite scores: (1) fluid cognition, (2) crystallized cognition, and (3) a total composite score. Brain structure was assessed through cortical morphology measured on a vertexwise and atlas-based region-of-interest (ROI) basis.
3D_Outcome.notes: Both cognition and brain are outcomes; brain is primary given the imaging focus.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Generalized additive mixed models for cognitive outcomes; linear mixed effects models (FEMA) for vertexwise brain analyses
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: age, sex, ethnicity, genetic ancestry, family ID/education/income/marital status, MRI device serial number, MRI software version, television viewing (in reading models) and reading (in TV models)
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: Generalized additive mixed models were constructed to determine the associations between daily pleasurable reading/television viewing and performance on the seven sub-tests and three composite metrics in the NIH Toolbox Cognition Battery. Given the demographic diversity in the sample, all statistical analyses controlled for age, sex, ethnicity, genetic ancestry, family ID/education/income/marital status, and geographical testing site. P-values were corrected for multiple comparisons using the Benjamini and Hochberg false discovery rate (FDR) procedure at an alpha level of 0.05.

3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Total Composite Score: Daily television viewing beta = -1.00 +/- 0.15, t-stat = -6.51, Q-value = 8.2e-10
Crystallized Cognition Composite Score: Daily television viewing beta = -0.87 +/- 0.15, t-stat = -5.51, Q-value = 1.9e-07
Fluid Cognition Composite Score: Daily television viewing beta = -0.80 +/- 0.16, t-stat = -4.83, Q-value = 3.5e-06
Picture Vocabulary: Daily television viewing beta = -0.56 +/- 0.15, t-stat = -3.75, Q-value = 0.00028
effect sizes for reading and television viewing on total cortical surface area were much smaller, accounting for just 0.2% and 0.02% of the total variance, respectively
Total Composite Score: Daily reading beta = 2.42 +/- 0.12, t-stat = 20.89, Q-value = 2.2e-94
Crystallized Cognition Composite Score: Daily reading beta = 2.45 +/- 0.089, t-stat = 27.58, Q-value = 5.4e-160

3G_Reporting_Quality.small_es_verbatim: At the whole-brain level, the size of the relationships between reading or television viewing and brain morphology are small, accounting for just 0.24% and 0.02% of the variance in total cortical surface area, respectively. Such small effects are not surprising given the regionally distributed profile of effects across the cortex at the vertexwise level and the myriad of factors that influence brain morphology.
3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: Paper carefully contextualizes effect sizes, comparing them to other established factors like parental income and education. Reports R2 differences for variance explained. The TV viewing effects on cognition are comparatively small (0.3-0.4% variance) versus reading effects (up to 8.4%).

3H_Recommendations.notes: Paper explicitly cautions against causal interpretation and does not issue clinical or policy recommendations.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.per_unit_verbatim: One hour of additional daily reading was associated with a 2.5-point increase in crystallized performance, and one hour of additional daily television viewing was associated with a 0.9-point decrease in crystallized performance, on a scale with a mean of 100 and standard deviation of 15.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion is notably cautious and measured. It emphasizes that TV viewing associations with cognition and brain morphology are comparatively small, contextualizes effect sizes against other factors (parental income, education), and explicitly warns against causal interpretation. The paper notes that reading effects substantially exceed TV viewing effects. The framing is scientific rather than alarmist, and does not suggest clinical action based on the findings.
